title: Coarea inequality with a 2-dimensional Jacobian: justify $\Vol_4(R)\ge\int_A\Vol_2(f^{-1}\Sigma_{a,b})\,da\,db$

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
The final integration step is asserted with a one-line justification:

> "Now using $\Dil_2(f)\le1$ and the coarea inequality, we have $R_1R_2R_3R_4=\Vol_4(R)\ge\int_A\Vol_2(f^{-1}(\Sigma_{a,b}))\,da\,db$."

This is **not** the standard coarea formula for a top-codimension submersion; here $f^{-1}\Sigma_{a,b}$ is a **2**-dimensional fibre of the composite $\phi=\pi_{34}\circ f:R\to[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]$, and one integrates the **2-volume** of the fibre against the $(a,b)$-parameter.

**What must be proved:** the coarea inequality in the form
$$\int_{B}\Vol_{n-m}(\phi^{-1}(t))\,dt\;\le\;\Dil_m(\phi)\,\Vol_n(M),\qquad \phi:M^n\to N^m,$$
applied with $n=4,m=2$, $\phi=\pi_{34}\circ f$, and the bound $\Dil_2(\pi_{34}\circ f)\le\Dil_2(f)\le1$. The direction of the inequality, the role of the $2$-dimensional Jacobian, and why $\Dil_2(f)\le1$ (rather than some Jacobian of $\phi$) provides the correct weight must all be verified carefully — it is easy to state this inequality with the wrong weight or direction (F3).
